claims.claim_text: The module opens with a detailed backstage anecdote tied to OpenAI's March 14, 2023 launch.
claims.span_hint: section: The Night That Changed Everything
claims.anchor_text: Sam Altman stood backstage at OpenAI's headquarters, watching the livestream numbers climb. In the next hour, they would demonstrate something that many researchers had thought was years away: an AI that could pass the bar exam, explain jokes, analyze images, and write working code from a photograph of a hand-drawn sketch.
claims.suggested_rewrite: OpenAI's March 2023 GPT-4 launch showcased a multimodal model that drew broad attention for its benchmark results and image-input capabilities.
claims.rationale: This is a cinematic narrative with precise scene details that are not attributable to an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: The launch supposedly involved teams working around the clock and engineers sleeping under desks.
claims.span_hint: section: The Night That Changed Everything
claims.anchor_text: What viewers didn't see was the chaos of the previous months. Teams working around the clock. Engineers sleeping under desks. A model so capable that even its creators weren't entirely sure what it could do.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large model launches often involve intense engineering work and substantial uncertainty about model behavior under real-world use.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war-story paragraph with false-precise backstage details.

claims.claim_text: The module ties a single 'gpt-5' release to Code Red at Google, Microsoft product integrations, Anthropic acceleration, and startup pivots.
claims.span_hint: section: The Night That Changed Everything
claims.anchor_text: When gpt-5 was released, the AI world fractured into before and after. Google declared "Code Red" and rushed their Bard release. Microsoft integrated gpt-5 into everything from Bing to Excel. Anthropic accelerated Claude development. And thousands of startups pivoted overnight—from building their own LLMs to building ON LLMs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The releases of ChatGPT and GPT-4 intensified competition across the AI industry and pushed many teams toward building applications on top of foundation models rather than training their own from scratch.
claims.rationale: This collapses several real but distinct events into an inaccurate single-release anecdote.

claims.claim_text: GPT-3 has 175 billion parameters and a printout of its parameters would stretch from New York to Los Angeles.
claims.span_hint: section: What Are Large Language Models? / Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: GPT-3 has 175 billion parameters. If you printed each parameter as a single digit, the printout would stretch from New York to Los Angeles!
claims.suggested_rewrite: GPT-3 has 175 billion parameters, which illustrates the enormous scale of frontier language models from that era.
claims.rationale: The parameter count is citable, but the printout-distance claim is an uncited derived statistic bundled into the same sentence.

claims.claim_text: The 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" introduced a transformer architecture based on attention and parallel processing.
claims.span_hint: section: The Transformer Revolution (2017)
claims.rationale: The paper directly describes the Transformer as an architecture based solely on attention mechanisms with improved parallelizability.

claims.claim_text: The transformer paper has 100,000+ citations and is one of the most cited ML papers ever.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Transformer Paper Was Almost Rejected
claims.anchor_text: - **100,000+ citations** (one of most cited ML papers ever)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - The paper became one of the most influential publications in modern machine learning.
claims.rationale: Citation counts drift over time and need a precise, current bibliometric source that is not present here.

claims.claim_text: Ashish Vaswani co-founded Essential AI with $50M+ funding.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Transformer Paper Was Almost Rejected
claims.anchor_text: - **Ashish Vaswani**: Co-founded Essential AI ($50M+ funding)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Ashish Vaswani** later co-founded a new AI startup.
claims.rationale: The funding figure is a time-sensitive company fact without an allowlisted primary citation here.

claims.claim_text: Illia Polosukhin co-founded NEAR Protocol at a $20B valuation.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Transformer Paper Was Almost Rejected
claims.anchor_text: - **Illia Polosukhin**: Co-founded NEAR Protocol ($20B valuation)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Illia Polosukhin** later co-founded NEAR Protocol.
claims.rationale: The valuation figure is an unsourced, time-sensitive quantitative business claim.

claims.claim_text: Jakob Uszkoreit co-founded Inceptive with $100M+ funding.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Transformer Paper Was Almost Rejected
claims.anchor_text: - **Jakob Uszkoreit**: Co-founded Inceptive ($100M+ funding)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Jakob Uszkoreit** later co-founded Inceptive.
claims.rationale: The funding figure is a specific business statistic without a cited allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: Meta supposedly released 'Llama 4' in July 2023 as a fully open-source 7B/13B/70B family.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? Meta Released Llama 4 for Free (and Changed Everything)
claims.anchor_text: Meta dropped a bombshell: **Llama 4** - a competitive LLM family (7B, 13B, 70B) - completely open-source.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Meta's 2023 open-weight Llama release accelerated interest in commercially usable open models.
claims.rationale: The model family, timing, and release characterization are inaccurate as written.

claims.claim_text: Llama had 30M+ downloads on Hugging Face in its first six months.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? Meta Released Llama 4 for Free (and Changed Everything)
claims.anchor_text: - **30M+ downloads** on Hugging Face
claims.suggested_rewrite: - The release quickly spawned a large ecosystem of downstream tools and fine-tuned variants.
claims.rationale: This is a time-sensitive platform statistic without a stable, allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: A later Llama release was trained on 15T tokens versus 2T for 'Llama 4'.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? Meta Released Llama 4 for Free (and Changed Everything)
claims.anchor_text: - Trained on 15T tokens (vs 2T for Llama 4)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Later frontier models were typically trained on substantially larger corpora than earlier open releases.
claims.rationale: This mixes an exact token-count comparison with an incorrect model reference.

claims.claim_text: gpt-5 is rumored to have 1.7T parameters.
claims.span_hint: section: Model Sizes and Capabilities / Example Sizes
claims.anchor_text: - gpt-5: Unknown (rumored 1.7T)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Frontier proprietary model sizes are often undisclosed publicly.
claims.rationale: A rumor-based parameter count is not a citable statistic.

claims.claim_text: A 10x parameter increase yields about 2x better performance.
claims.span_hint: section: Does Size Matter? / Scaling Laws
claims.anchor_text: - 10x more parameters → ~2x better performance
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Scaling-law research found that adding parameters, data, and compute tends to improve performance predictably, but the returns follow power laws rather than a simple rule of thumb.
claims.rationale: This exact multiplier is an oversimplified numerical claim that is not stated that way in the cited scaling-laws work.

claims.claim_text: Claude has a 200K context while gpt-5 has 8K/32K context.
claims.span_hint: section: Does Size Matter? / But
claims.anchor_text: - Longer context helps: Claude's 200K context vs gpt-5's 8K/32K
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Context-window limits vary significantly by provider, model, and release, so check current documentation before making architecture decisions.
claims.rationale: These provider-specific limits are version-sensitive and the OpenAI side is not reliably supported as written.

claims.claim_text: RLHF trains models from human preference rankings to produce more preferred outputs.
claims.span_hint: section: Fine-tuning: The Specialization / RLHF
claims.rationale: The InstructGPT paper directly describes training with human feedback and preference modeling.

claims.claim_text: Training GPT-3 cost $4.6 million in GPU costs.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? GPT-3's Training Cost Would Bankrupt Most Startups
claims.anchor_text: - **$4.6 million** in GPU costs (Lambda Labs estimate)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Training a frontier model from scratch can cost millions of dollars in compute.
claims.rationale: This is an estimate from a non-allowlisted secondary source and should not be presented as a hard number.

claims.claim_text: gpt-5 training reportedly cost $50-100 million.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? GPT-3's Training Cost Would Bankrupt Most Startups
claims.anchor_text: - Rumored cost: **$50-100 million**
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Training costs for frontier proprietary models are rarely disclosed publicly and are widely believed to be very high.
claims.rationale: Rumored exact training-cost numbers are not citable facts.

claims.claim_text: Anthropic was founded by OpenAI's own safety team.
claims.span_hint: section: The Anthropic Origin Story: AI Safety Rebels
claims.anchor_text: **Anthropic** wasn't founded by outsiders—it was founded by **OpenAI's own safety team**.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Anthropic** was founded by former OpenAI researchers and positioned itself around AI safety and reliability.
claims.rationale: The 'own safety team' wording is too specific and stronger than the cited company sources cleanly support.

claims.claim_text: Constitutional AI trains models using explicit principles and AI feedback rather than only keyword filtering.
claims.span_hint: section: The Anthropic Origin Story: AI Safety Rebels
claims.rationale: Anthropic's Constitutional AI paper directly describes this training approach.

claims.claim_text: More than 200 LLMs were launched in China in 2023 alone.
claims.span_hint: section: The Chinese AI Awakening (2023-2024)
claims.anchor_text: - **200+** LLMs launched in China in 2023 alone
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Chinese companies released many competing large-language-model products in 2023 and 2024.
claims.rationale: This exact count is a time-sensitive market statistic with no cited primary source.

claims.claim_text: Mistral 7B outperformed 'Llama 4 13B'.
claims.span_hint: section: The Mistral Miracle: 3 Months to $2 Billion
claims.anchor_text: - **Outperformed Llama 4 13B** (almost twice its size!)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Outperformed Llama 2 13B** on the benchmarks reported in the Mistral 7B paper.
claims.rationale: The benchmark comparison is tied to the wrong Llama generation.

claims.claim_text: Mistral raised $415M at a $2B valuation in December 2023, six months after founding.
claims.span_hint: section: The Mistral Miracle: 3 Months to $2 Billion
claims.anchor_text: - **December 2023**: $415M raised at **$2B valuation**—just 6 months after founding!
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Mistral's rapid fundraising and model releases made it one of the most closely watched AI startups of 2023.
claims.rationale: This exact fundraising-and-valuation claim needs a primary financing source and is too specific to leave uncited.

claims.claim_text: Gemini offers very long context windows, including million-token-class context on supported models.
claims.span_hint: section: Proprietary Models / Google Gemini; section: Context Windows
claims.rationale: Google Cloud documentation directly describes Gemini long-context offerings and million-token-scale context windows.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI says API data is not used for training by default and abuse-monitoring logs are retained for up to 30 days.
claims.span_hint: section: Privacy and Security Considerations / Data Usage Policies
claims.rationale: The OpenAI data-controls page directly states both the default training policy and the 30-day abuse-monitoring retention period.

claims.claim_text: The crossover point between API and self-hosted use is roughly 1M tokens per month.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Takeaways / API vs Self-hosted Trade-offs
claims.anchor_text: - The crossover point is roughly 1M tokens/month
claims.suggested_rewrite: - The API-versus-self-hosting crossover point depends on model choice, utilization, hardware costs, and operational overhead.
claims.rationale: This is a context-dependent cost heuristic presented as a universal threshold.

claims.claim_text: Llama 4 70B competes with GPT-3.5.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Misconceptions / Myth 4
claims.anchor_text: **Reality**: Gap narrowing fast. Llama 4 70B competes with GPT-3.5. For many tasks, open-source is good enough.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Reality**: The gap between open and proprietary models has narrowed, and open models are good enough for many practical tasks.
claims.rationale: This specific cross-model benchmark claim uses the wrong Llama generation and needs a more careful benchmark reference.

claims.claim_text: The Mistral 7B paper reports Apache 2.0 licensing and benchmark wins over Llama 2 13B.
claims.span_hint: section: Open-Source Models / Mistral AI
claims.rationale: The paper directly states the Apache 2.0 license and reports outperforming Llama 2 13B on its evaluated benchmarks.

claims.claim_text: Anthropic documents a 200K token context window for standard Claude API usage.
claims.span_hint: section: Context Windows
claims.rationale: Anthropic's context-window documentation explicitly describes a 200K-token standard context window.

claims.claim_text: GPT-3.5 has 175B parameters.
claims.span_hint: section: The LLM Landscape (2024-2025) / OpenAI GPT Family
claims.anchor_text: - **GPT-3.5**: 175B parameters, cheap, fast
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **GPT-3.5**: an earlier, lower-cost GPT model that was widely used before newer releases.
claims.rationale: The 175B figure is associated with GPT-3, not a clearly documented GPT-3.5 model size.

claims.claim_text: gpt-5 is OpenAI's largest, most capable multimodal model.
claims.span_hint: section: The LLM Landscape (2024-2025) / OpenAI GPT Family
claims.anchor_text: - **gpt-5**: Largest (size unknown), multimodal, most capable
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Frontier GPT models**: proprietary multimodal systems whose exact internal sizes are typically undisclosed.
claims.rationale: This is a moving, vendor-specific capability ranking that should not be frozen in an uncited curriculum module.

claims.claim_text: gpt-5 Turbo is a faster, cheaper version of gpt-5.
claims.span_hint: section: The LLM Landscape (2024-2025) / OpenAI GPT Family
claims.anchor_text: - **gpt-5 Turbo**: Faster, cheaper gpt-5
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Smaller GPT variants**: usually trade some capability for lower cost and lower latency.
claims.rationale: This specific product naming and positioning is version-sensitive and should not be left uncited.

further_reading.title: Attention Is All You Need
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the foundational transformer paper for understanding the architecture behind modern LLMs.

further_reading.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
further_reading.why_relevant: This paper introduced GPT-3 and is a key reference for scale, few-shot prompting, and early frontier-model behavior.

further_reading.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
further_reading.why_relevant: This paper is the canonical reference for instruction tuning and RLHF, which shaped modern chat assistants.

notes: The module contains many bundled 2023-2025 product, pricing, and funding claims; several markdown tables and model-family bullet lists will likely need a manual freshness pass in addition to citation injection because some rows are stale, duplicated, or mix correct concepts with incorrect model names.
